preamble: The shift from the voiced alveolar lateral fricative [ɮ] to the voiceless postalveolar fricative [ʃ] (sh) is a form of sibilantization and devoicing. This process involves the loss of lateral airflow and voicing, typically occurring as part of a merger into the existing sibilant inventory.

examples.note: In some peripheral Arabic dialects, the historical lateral fricative (Dad) merged with sibilants rather than becoming [dˤ].

sources: Holes, C. (2004). Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
